title: कबीर दोहे (मुख्य दोहे)
devanagariText:
॥ श्री कबीर दोहे - मुख्य दोहे ॥

गुरु गोविन्द दोउ खड़े, काके लागूँ पाय।
बलिहारी गुरु आपनो, गोविन्द दियो बताय॥१॥

ऐसी वाणी बोलिए, मन का आपा खोय।
औरन को शीतल करे, आपहु शीतल होय॥२॥

बड़ा हुआ तो क्या हुआ, जैसे पेड़ खजूर।
पंथी को छाया नहीं, फल लागे अति दूर॥३॥

निंदक नियरे राखिए, आँगन कुटी छवाय।
बिन पानी साबुन बिना, निर्मल करे सुभाय॥४॥

बुरा जो देखन मैं चला, बुरा न मिलिया कोय।
जो दिल खोजा आपना, मुझसे बुरा न कोय॥५॥

दुःख में सुमिरन सब करें, सुख में करे न कोय।
जो सुख में सुमिरन करे, तो दुःख काहे को होय॥६॥

काल करे सो आज कर, आज करे सो अब।
पल में परलय होएगी, बहुरि करेगो कब॥७॥

पोथी पढ़ पढ़ जग मुआ, पंडित भया न कोय।
ढाई आखर प्रेम का, पढ़े सो पंडित होय॥८॥

जैसा भोजन खाइये, तैसा ही मन होय।
जैसा पानी पीजिए, तैसी वाणी होय॥९॥

माटी कहे कुम्हार से, तू क्या रौंदे मोय।
एक दिन ऐसा आएगा, मैं रौंदूँगी तोय॥१०॥

जहाँ दया तहाँ धर्म है, जहाँ लोभ तहाँ पाप।
जहाँ क्रोध तहाँ काल है, जहाँ क्षमा तहाँ आप॥११॥

साईं इतना दीजिए, जा में कुटुम समाय।
मैं भी भूखा न रहूँ, साधु न भूखा जाय॥१२॥

चलती चक्की देख कर, दिया कबीरा रोय।
दो पाटन के बीच में, साबुत बचा न कोय॥१३॥

तिनका कबहुँ ना निन्दिये, जो पाँव तले होय।
कबहुँ उड़ आँखों पड़े, पीर घनेरी होय॥१४॥

धीरे-धीरे रे मना, धीरे सब कुछ होय।
माली सींचे सौ घड़ा, ऋतु आए फल होय॥१५

॥ इति श्री कबीर दोहे मुख्य दोहे सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Kabir Dohe - Key Dohe ||

Guru Govind dou khade, kake lagu paay.
Balihari Guru aapno, Govind diyo batay.||1||

Aisi vani boliye, man ka aapa khoy.
Auran ko sheetal kare, aapahu sheetal hoy.||2||

Bada hua to kya hua, jaise ped khajur.
Panthi ko chhaya nahi, phal lage ati dur.||3||

Nindak niyare rakhiye, aangan kuti chhavay.
Bin pani sabun bina, nirmal kare subhay.||4||

Bura jo dekhan main chala, bura na miliya koy.
Jo dil khoja aapna, mujhse bura na koy.||5||

Dukh mein sumiran sab karein, sukh mein kare na koy.
Jo sukh mein sumiran kare, to dukh kahe ko hoy.||6||

Kaal kare so aaj kar, aaj kare so ab.
Pal mein parlay hoegi, bahuri karego kab.||7||

Pothi padh padh jag mua, pandit bhaya na koy.
Dhai aakhar prem ka, padhe so pandit hoy.||8||

Jaisa bhojan khaiye, taisa hi man hoy.
Jaisa paani peejiye, taisi vaani hoy.||9||

Maati kahe kumhar se, tu kya raundhe moy.
Ek din aisa aayega, main raundungi toy.||10||

Jahan daya tahan dharm hai, jahan lobh tahan paap.
Jahan krodh tahan kaal hai, jahan kshama tahan aap.||11||

Sain itna dijiye, ja mein kutum samay.
Main bhi bhukha na rahun, sadhu na bhukha jay.||12||

Chalti chakki dekh kar, diya Kabira roy.
Do paatan ke beech mein, sabut bacha na koy.||13||

Tinka kabahu na nindiye, jo paav tale hoy.
Kabahu ud aankhon pade, peer ghaneri hoy.||14||

Dheere-dheere re mana, dheere sab kuchh hoy.
Maali sinche sau ghada, ritu aaye phal hoy.||15||

|| Iti Shri Kabir Dohe Key Dohe Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: कबीर दास जी के ये प्रसिद्ध दोहे जीवन के गहन सत्य सिखाते हैं। गुरु और गोविन्द में गुरु को प्रथम वन्दन, क्योंकि गुरु ने ही गोविन्द का मार्ग बताया। मीठी वाणी बोलो जो सबको शीतल करे। निंदक को पास रखो क्योंकि वह बिना साबुन-पानी स्वभाव निर्मल करता है। बुराई ढूँढने चला तो बुरा कोई न मिला, दिल में खोजा तो मुझसे बुरा कोई नहीं। काल करे सो आज कर - समय का सदुपयोग करो। ढाई अक्षर प्रेम का पढ़े सो पंडित - प्रेम ही सच्ची विद्या है।

Teaches humility, compassion and devotion to God

Accessible spiritual wisdom for people of all backgrounds
description: Dedicated to Lord Vishnu, the Kabir Dohe Key Verses (कबीर दोहे (मुख्य दोहे)) is a sacred prayer revered in Hindu tradition. This sacred composition is sourced from Kabir Granthavali and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to practical life lessons on speech, time management and love as well as accessible spiritual wisdom for people of all backgrounds. Additionally, it is known to emphasize guru bhakti and inner transformation. Recitation is particularly auspicious during Guru Purnima. With 15 verses and a reading time of about 8 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Kabir Dohe Key Verses in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Kabir Dohe - famous couplets of Sant Kabir in Hindi with meaning. Timeless wisdom on guru bhakti, humility, love and spiritual growth.

seoFaqs.question: What are Kabir ke Dohe and why are they so famous?
seoFaqs.answer: Kabir ke Dohe (कबीर के दोहे) are the celebrated two-line Hindi couplets composed by Sant Kabir Das (1398/1440–1518 CE) — the great Bhakti-saint, weaver-mystic of Varanasi, foundational figure of the Sant Mat tradition and equally claimed by Hindus, Muslims and Sikhs. The dohas are written in plain colloquial Awadhi-Bhojpuri Hindi (deliberately rejecting Sanskrit Brahmanical orthodoxy) using everyday metaphors (the spinning wheel, the weaver's loom, the millstone, the water-pot) to convey the deepest spiritual truths. Each doha is self-contained, easily memorized, and accessible to even illiterate listeners — making them the most-quoted Hindi devotional verses across all communities for over six centuries. They constitute the heart of the Kabir Granthavali, the Bijak (his canonical Hindu-tradition collection) and are also included in the Sikh Guru Granth Sahib (where Kabir's hymns occupy 292 padas). (Sources: Kabir Granthavali; Bijak; Guru Granth Sahib.)

seoFaqs.question: Who was Sant Kabir Das?
seoFaqs.answer: Sant Kabir Das (कबीर दास) (variously dated 1398–1518 CE, lived primarily in 15th-century Varanasi) was a Bhakti-saint, mystic-poet, weaver by profession, and the foremost figure of the Nirguna Bhakti (formless-divine devotion) tradition. According to legend he was born of a Brahmin widow, abandoned at a riverbank, and adopted by the Muslim weaver couple Niru and Nima — making him simultaneously claimed by Hindus and Muslims, a paradox he famously embraced. He was the disciple of the Vaishnava saint Ramananda (who chose him by accidentally pronouncing Ram-Ram on the bathing ghat where Kabir had hidden). His teachings systematically rejected: (1) caste hierarchy, (2) idol worship, (3) ritualism (yajna, hajj, tirtha), (4) all sectarianism (Hindu-Muslim binary), (5) book-learning without inner realization. He preached: (a) Nirguna Brahman (formless God), (b) Guru-bhakti, (c) inward silence (Anhad nada), (d) Ram-naam japa as the sole sadhana. (Sources: Kabir hagiography; Sant Mat tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the most famous Kabir dohe and their meanings?
seoFaqs.answer: Among Kabir's most-loved dohas: (1) Guru Govind dou khade kake lagu paay, balihari guru aapno Govind diyo batay — When both Guru and God stand before me, whom should I bow to first? I bow to my Guru first because it is he who showed me Govind. (2) Bura jo dekhan main chala bura na miliya koy, jo dil khoja aapna mujhse bura na koy — When I went looking for the bad, I found none; when I searched my own heart, none was worse than I. (3) Dheere-dheere re mana, dheere sab kuchh hoy, mali sinche sau ghada ritu aaye phal hoy — Slowly, O mind, slowly all things happen; the gardener may pour a hundred pots, but fruit comes only when the season comes. (4) Pothi padh padh jag mua pandit bhaya na koy, dhai aakhar prem ka padhe so pandit hoy — Reading books the world died, none became wise; whoever reads the two-and-a-half letters of Prem (love) becomes wise. (Source: Kabir Granthavali.)

seoFaqs.question: What spiritual lessons do Kabir's dohe teach?
seoFaqs.answer: Kabir's dohas form a complete practical spirituality covering: (1) Guru bhakti — supreme reverence for the awakened guru above even God (doha 1); (2) Ahimsa of speech — speak words that cool both speaker and hearer (Aisi vani boliye doha); (3) Inner reform over outward show — accept critics warmly as they polish character without soap or water (Nindak niyare rakhiye doha); (4) Self-examination — the worst person is found within, not without (Bura jo dekhan doha); (5) Time-discipline — Kal kare so aaj kar (do tomorrow's task today, today's now); (6) Love over learning — two-and-a-half letters of Prem outweigh all books; (7) Right diet — food shapes the mind, water shapes the speech; (8) Death-mindfulness — the clay reminds the potter that one day it will trample him; (9) Compassion economics — give enough that family eats and the holy man does not go hungry; (10) Patience — fruit comes only in season. (Sources: Kabir Bijak; Kabir Granthavali; Sakhi collection.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should Kabir's Dohe be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Kabir Dohe have no specific tithi-restriction (their universalism rejects ritual fixity) but are most powerfully recited on: Guru Purnima (Ashadha Purnima — the day of Guru-veneration, when the Guru-Govind doha is universally chanted), Kabir Jayanti (Jyeshtha Purnima — Kabir's birth anniversary, observed by Kabir Panthis worldwide), every morning as wake-up satsang reading (just one or two dohas with reflection), at evening sandhya as contemplation material, before sleep as moral self-examination, at any moral crossroads (the Bura jo dekhan doha), at funerals and shraddha (the Maati kahe kumhar doha on death), in school and home moral education (every Hindi-medium child learns Kabir dohas), in inter-faith satsangs (where Kabir's universalism resolves Hindu-Muslim tension), and during Sant Samagams. They form part of the daily Sikh prayer (Nitnem) where Kabir's padas are chanted at Sukhmani Sahib gatherings. (Sources: Kabir Panth tradition; Sant Mat practice.)

seoFaqs.question: How are Kabir's dohas different from classical Sanskrit stotras?
seoFaqs.answer: Kabir's dohas differ fundamentally from classical Sanskrit stotras in form, language, theology and audience. (1) Form: Dohas are 2-line Hindi couplets in 24+24 matras (rhythm) — vs. multi-verse Sanskrit shlokas in classical chhanda (anushtup, indravajra etc.); (2) Language: Plain colloquial Awadhi-Bhojpuri Hindi accessible to illiterate masses — vs. Sanskrit accessible only to Brahmin scholars; (3) Theology: Nirguna Brahman (formless, beyond name-form, beyond Hindu-Muslim labels) — vs. Saguna devotion to Vishnu, Shiva, Devi as personal forms; (4) Ritual stance: Active rejection of yajna, mantra-japa, idol-worship, hajj, tirtha — vs. ritual-prescriptive stotras that mandate specific upasana; (5) Audience: Universal, especially the marginalized weavers, jolahas, scheduled-castes and Muslims — vs. Sanskrit stotras targeted at Brahmin-Kshatriya twice-born. Kabir's dohas thus represent the Bhakti-rebellion against Brahmanical orthodoxy, parallel to Surdas, Mirabai and the Tamil Alvars in their respective regions. (Sources: Bhakti movement scholarship; Sant Mat tradition.)
